g.data-narration: Welcome to Exception Handling in C++. This module covers try-catch-throw mechanism for robust error handling.
Traditional error handling uses return codes scattered throughout code, making it hard to read and maintain.
The exception handling mechanism has three pillars: try contains risky code, throw signals errors, catch handles them.
g.text: The Three Pillars of Exception Handling

g.data-narration: The basic syntax wraps potentially throwing code in try, followed immediately by catch handlers that specify exception types.
When code in try throws an exception, control transfers to the matching catch block, skipping any intermediate code.
Multiple catch blocks handle different exception types. Order matters: catch derived classes before base classes for proper matching.
C++ provides a standard exception hierarchy. Know this hierarchy: std::exception is the root, with logic_error and runtime_error as key branches.
Stack unwinding destroys local objects when exceptions propagate, ensuring proper cleanup. Local objects are destroyed, heap objects persist.
A catch block can rethrow the same exception using throw; without arguments. This passes the exception to outer handlers.
Create custom exceptions by deriving from std::exception. Override the what() method to provide meaningful error messages.
When constructors throw, the object is never fully created and its destructor won't be called. Use smart pointers or careful cleanup.
g.text: ✓ Solution: Use smart pointers for heap resources or try-catch inside constructor

g.data-narration: Destructors should never throw - if thrown during stack unwinding, std::terminate is called. Use noexcept to promise no exceptions.
The noexcept specifier promises a function won't throw. If it does, std::terminate is called immediately. Enables compiler optimizations.
Always catch by const reference, order catch blocks from specific to general, use catch(...) as last resort.
Avoid catching by value (causes slicing), wrong catch order, empty catch blocks, and throwing from destructors.
Exception handling in C++ provides robust error management through try-catch-throw. Use it properly for reliable, maintainable code.
g.text: Master Exception Handling for Robust C++ Programs
